one_line_takeaway: A retraining-free prompt framework combining verbal-confidence elicitation, explicit partial reward for 'I don't know', and humility norms cuts GPT-5 mini's surfaced-answer hallucination rate on PopQA from 52.3% to 34.2% by trading coverage for reliability along a tunable abstention-hallucination frontier, without improving forced-answer accuracy.
key_finding: On the main setting (GPT-5 mini, PopQA), rewarding abstention and adding norms monotonically lowers the false-answer rate among surfaced answers by shifting error-prone cases to abstention, but this trades coverage for reliability while leaving forced-answer accuracy essentially unchanged (FAR_overall overlaps across schemes). FAR_answered falls from 52.3% (Pure Eval) to 48.2% (Scheme A) to 41.0% (Scheme B) to 34.2% (Scheme B with norms), while Coverage drops from 96.5% to 84.0% to 67.9% to 55.3%. The improvement is thus movement along a stable abstention-hallucination frontier (a deployment control knob), not improved underlying factual competence. Ablation shows a division of labor: reward framing drives abstention (reward-only collapses coverage to 29.8%, over-abstaining), while verbal confidence keeps abstention from becoming overly aggressive. Behavior is far more sensitive to the abstention reward than to the correct-answer reward or false-answer penalty.
core_contribution: I-CALM is a prompt-only (no retraining, black-box) framework for reducing LLM factual hallucination via confidence-aware abstention. Its three coupled, own components are: (i) eliciting self-reported verbal confidence tau_self in [0,1] through a two-stage abstention-conditioned prompt (answer+confidence, or say 'I don't know' then give a best guess+confidence); (ii) announcing an explicit answer/abstain reward scheme that gives partial credit +gamma to 'I don't know'; and (iii) adding five lightweight normative principles emphasizing truthfulness, humility, and responsibility. The paper's contribution is not new abstention mechanisms per se (training-time [IDK] tokens, abstention RL, and risk-control wrappers are cited as prior/related work, not claimed here) but the demonstration that a purely prompt-level incentive+norm framing, paired with verbal confidence, shifts answer/abstain behavior and reduces false-answer rate among surfaced answers, tracing a tunable abstention-hallucination frontier.
threat_model: N/A - not a security paper. There is no adversary, attack, or malicious input. The paper addresses an epistemic-reliability failure (models confidently emitting false answers to factual questions with a verifiable ground truth) driven by benchmark scoring incentives that reward guessing over abstaining. No threat model should be inferred; the setting is honest uncertainty / selective prediction, not attack-defense.
limitations: (1) Gains are selective-answering only: FAR_overall / forced-answer accuracy is largely unchanged, so no improvement in underlying knowledge. (2) Reduced FAR comes at a substantial coverage cost (down to 55.3% with norms on GPT-5 mini). (3) Effect magnitude is heterogeneous across models and datasets and transfers most clearly only where there is high first-round hallucination risk to remove. (4) Self-reported verbal confidence is imperfectly calibrated (Brier ~0.33-0.35, non-trivial ECE) and models do not follow the Bayes-optimal threshold (tau=0.7 under Scheme B) - they keep answering across the confidence range. (5) Scope is limited to factual questions with a verifiable answer, not genuinely unanswerable/underspecified queries. (6) Behavior may drift under domain shift or multi-turn interaction. (7) GPT-5 mini does not expose token log-probabilities, so the confidence-baseline validation was done on GPT-4o mini.

datasets_used: PopQA (main; 14,267 factual questions built from Wikidata knowledge triples)
quantitative_results: GPT-5 mini / PopQA FAR_answered: Pure Eval 52.3%, Scheme A 48.2%, Scheme B 41.0%, Scheme B with norms 34.2%
GPT-5 mini / PopQA Coverage: Pure Eval 96.5%, Scheme A 84.0%, Scheme B 67.9%, Scheme B with norms 55.3%
FAR_overall (forced-answer) remains similar across schemes with overlapping 95% CIs
Total reward: Scheme B with norms 5039.6 vs Scheme B 3577.2
Pure Eval AER upper bound at most 5.8% (single-round, model rarely signals uncertainty)
Scheme B with norms converts ~60% of Pure-Eval-incorrect cases into abstentions, leaving ~30% still first-round incorrect

Ablation (Scheme B +1,-1,+0.4, GPT-5 mini): Confidence-only Coverage 0.860 / AER 0.214 / FAR_answered 0.487; Reward-only Coverage 0.298 / AER 0.868 / FAR_answered 0.243; Full Scheme B Coverage 0.679 / AER 0.499 / FAR_answered 0.410
On the reward-only ablation's answered subset, full Scheme B still achieves lower FAR (0.197 vs 0.243)
Confidence validation (GPT-4o mini / PopQA, 4 templates): tau_self vs tau_token_avg Pearson r ~0.54; Brier scores ~0.33-0.35 for both signals

Extreme-reward sensitivity: scaling abstain reward 0.4->40 shifts Coverage to 0.428, FAR_answered to 0.283, AER to 0.778; scaling correct reward 1->100 or penalty -1->-100 yields only marginal changes
Bayes-optimal answer/abstain threshold tau=(gamma+beta)/(R+beta) = 0.7 under Scheme B (+1,-1,+0.4); model empirically does not implement this sharp cutoff
baselines_compared: Pure Eval (direct prompting with no reward framing or verbal confidence) - primary baseline
Post-hoc geometric-mean token-probability confidence tau_token_avg (uncertainty-signal baseline)
Scheme A (+R correct, -beta incorrect; no explicit abstention reward) vs Scheme B (adds +gamma for IDK) vs Scheme B with norms
Component ablations: confidence-only and reward-only vs full Scheme B
Cross-model comparison: GPT-5 mini, GPT-4o mini, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507
